data.value_notes: Uses Sunset zones which are not necessarily the equivalent of hardiness zones.

What the deer resistance numbers mean:
1-2=They will eat this plant in one night, many times not even leaving the roots. If you have ever even seen a deer in the area it is best not to plant these plants.
3-4=If you have these plants in your garden you don't have deer, they will leave a leafless, stemless stock that usually will recover poorly. Roses and fruit trees fit into this bracket.
5-6=In light deer populations they will not bother, in moderate populations the plants will be eaten like numbers 3-4, in heavy populations they will remove, stems and all.
7=These plants will tolerate moderate deer populations, heavy populations may be disfigured or damaged. Many native shrubs fit into this category. If under some drought stress (as they should be) deer will prefer your neighbors plants. Some non-natives like rock roses and sun roses are in at this number also. If you have rock roses and they are not being eaten then #7's should be ok.
8-9=These plants are safe in 99% of California and they generally recover in the worst of areas.( Look at note in 10 about new plantings.) Many of the 8-10's are safe for years and a drought will bring the deer population to a higher threshold. Then your 8-9's will be eaten but your neighbors 3-5's will disappear.
10=Close to perfect, sometimes a stupid deer doesn't know better. We have trouble with these when they are planted because the root system will not hold the plant into the ground when the deer check them out. They pull them out. That is why we recommend a cage for the first year (read on). They still will eat the new growth (2-3") on bad years. 
?=A question mark after the number means the author is not confident in the score assigned.
M=the source said it has moderate deer resistance
VH=very high deer resistance\nH=high deer resistance

data.oregon_description: Yellow flag is a very showy species growing 3-4 feet in height with the most vigorous growth attained in the wettest environments. The leaves are long, flattened and sword-like, typical of most iris. Large plant clumps are formed from the lateral growth of rhizomes sometimes attaining 20 feet in width. Flowers are produced on erect plant stalks with multiple flowers produced on each. Fruit capsules are large, 3-angled and up to 4 inches in length. Disk-like seeds are shed from the capsules throughout the fall and winter. Floating mats of seed can be observed in backwaters and marshes aiding dispersal.

data.oregon_description: Poison hemlock is a biennial member of the carrot family, growing from 3 to 7 feet tall on a deep taproot. Hollow stems are erect with leaves that are alternate, one per node, petioled, and pinnately divided. Flowers are white in compound stemmed umbels, much like little umbrellas. data.oregon_description: English hawthorn is a broadly spreading shrub or small tree growing up to 25’ tall. The trunk has pale, gray smooth bark and younger stems bear sharp thorns making the plant useful for natural fence plantings in pastures. The leaves are 1-2 inches long and deeply lobed, sometimes almost to the midrib, with the lobes spreading out like a birds foot. Flowers are produced in showy white clumps from late spring to early summer in clumps of 5-25 blossoms; each flower is about 1 cm diameter. Pollinated flowers produce a haw, which is a small, oval, dark red fruit about 1 cm long, berry-like, but structurally a pome containing a single seed.

data.oregon_description: Paterson’s curse is an erect annual (less often biennial) in the Borage plant family. Plants can be single-stemmed or multi-branched with an abundance of stout hairs on stems and leaves. Flowers are most often blue-purple in color, but may be pink or white. Flowers are borne on fiddleneck or scorpioid-like inflorescences. Two of the five stamens in the flower are longer and project significantly from the joined corolla. In Oregon, blooming starts as early as March and continues through June. Reproduction and spread is by seed. data.oregon_description: Taurian thistle is a vigorous biennial, or short-lived perennial with coarse, spiny leaves and conspicuous spiny-winged stems. The plants are a nearly florescent lime-green in color with large, mostly singular flower heads at the terminals of the main and side stems. The bright purple flower heads are 3 to 4 inches in diameter. The heads consist of numerous spiny-tipped bracts resembling an artichoke before the bud opens. The leaves are typically covered with short, sticky-glandular hairs, acutely triangular, with 6 to 8 pairs of spiny-toothed lobes. Taurian thistle seedlings typically appear after the first fall rains and develop into large rosettes the next growing season. From this rosette and taproot an 8 foot tall plant develops. Dead stems can persist into the next season with spines attached. Taurian thistle reproduces only by seed. Most seeds germinate in the fall but can germinate through out the summer. Buried seed can remain viable in the soil seed bank for at least 7 years and possibly to 20 years or more. Wind, water, animals, and vehicles can disperse seeds.

There are other non-native blackberry species in Oregon. Armenian is the most robust.

data.oregon_description: Milk thistle is a biennial or winter annual blooming from April through July. It grows two to six feet tall. Stems are stout, rigid and generally branching. Leaves are very broad and clasp the flower stem. The spiny margins and white marbling along veins are very distinctive. Flower heads are reddish-purple, spine-tipped and 3-4 inches across. The seed is capable of remaining dormant in the soil for many years.

data.oregon_description: It is a small to medium sized perennial ornamental grass or tall non-traditional groundcover. It grows to 1' tall in full sun, 1.5' tall in partial sun, and up to 3' tall in partial shade, spreading continuously at its perimeter to form a dense groundcover procumbent mat. Clumps have slow to medium growth rates at their perimeter. The best growth performance occurs in moist, well-drained soils in partial sun, but it is very tolerant of poor soils. Leaves are ¾ to 1 inch wide and 5 to 7 inches long. Lanceolate to narrow ovate leaves emerge briefly with pink and white variegation, but quickly change to longitudinal stripes (or ribbons) of green and white variegation as they expand (hence the common names of Garden's Garters, resembling striped garters or suspenders). Flowers are rare or sparse, white to pink and display in June and July, on a 2' stalk. Plants bleach to beige with the first frosts. Foliage remains attractive in winter.

data.definition: A woody, generally multi-stemmed plant that loses its leaves and becomes dormant during the winter. WSU

A tree that sheds its leaves at the end of a growing season and regrows them at the beginning of the next growing season. Most deciduous trees bear flowers and have woody stems and broad, rather than needle-like, leaves. WSU
Deer will eat any plant if hungry enough but if general forage options are favorable, they usually don't eat the plant identified as "deer-resistant".
A period of relatively long duration with substantially below-normal precipitation, usually occurring over a large area (WSU)

Drought tolerance is the ability to which a plant maintains its biomass production during arid or drought conditions. (Wikipedia)\n "Generally speaking, there are three major strategies employed by plants to survive annual drought conditions: desiccation tolerance; drought avoidance; and drought tolerance." https://ucanr.edu/blogs/blogcore/postdetail.cfm?postnum=28129
A woody, multi-stemmed shrub that retains its leaves throughout the year. WSU

Flammability refers to the ability of a plant to ignite and transfer heat and/or flames to surrounding plants or structures. Plants are flammable for different reasons; some plants are highly ignitable, but burn quickly. Other plants are not easily ignitable, but can burn for a long time once ignited. Flammability is comprised of four components:
Ignitability: The time to ignition once exposed to an ignition source such as an ember or flame.
Sustainability: The duration a plant will sustain a fire.
Combustibility: The rate of spread and rate of heat (or energy) given off from a burning plant.
Consumability: The percent of a plant consumed in a fire. (https://edis.ifas.ufl.edu/publication/fr151)

Plants that are "flammable" generally have several of these characteristics: contain fine, dry, or dead material within the plants, such as twigs, needles, and leaves; leaves, twigs, and stems contain volatile waxes, terpenes, or oils; leaves are aromatic (strong odor when crushed). OSU 2006  The flammability of a plant is reduced if it contains a lot of salt, soap, latex, and/or pectins. (Idaho Firewise)

Fire-reluctant plants are those that are slow to ignite when exposed to a heat source and once ignited are slow to propagate fire spread. A "fire resistant plant" can be defined the same, but in fire ecology, fire resistance refers to a plant that is adapted to fire by protective features such as thick bark or has the ability to reestablish after fire.\nFire-reluctant and fire resistant plants usually have high moisture content, high salt and saponin content, and low volatile oil and resin content. Other characteristics that contribute to flammability are age, size, total volume, and amount of dead material retained within and under the plant.

(1) High fire resistance means decreased flammability; (2) a species with….less flammable foliage that has a relatively lower probability of being killed or scarred by a fire than a fire sensitive [plant]. (National Wildfire Coordinating Group). (3) so resistant to fire that for a specified time and under conditions of a standard heat intensity it will not fail structurally or allow transit of heat and will not permit the side away from the fire to become hotter than a specified temperature. (Merriam Webster)\n"Fire-resistant plants are those that do not readily ignite from a flame or other ignition sources.  ...they tend to produce fewer embers, the ost common causefor homes burning in wildfires.  Their foliage and stems do not significantly contribute to fuel and fire intensity." (OSU, October 2023).
See "fire-reluctant" and "fire-resistant" plant.These plants are slower to ignite when exposed to heat and usually do not burn quickly. When they burn, they release energy more slowly.

A low-growing or trailing group of plants usually used to cover bare earth and create a uniform appearance. (WSU)   Groundcover provides protection of the topsoil from erosion and drought. In an ecosystem, the ground cover forms the layer of vegetation below the shrub layer known as the herbaceous layer. Wikipedia

A hardiness zone is a geographic area defined to encompass a certain range of climatic conditions relevant to plant growth and survival. The original and most widely used system was developed by the United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) as a rough guide for landscaping and gardening. The hardiness zones for the Rogue Valley are 7a,b and 8 a,b as evident on the USDA Plant Hardiness Map for Oregon https://www.gardeningknowhow.com/wp-content/uploads/2004/10/oregon_map+_lg.gif .
For a more thorough discussion on the hardiness zones attributed in this spreadsheet go to page 5 the OSU Fire-resistant Plants for Home Landscapes (ref 2). 
According to OSU's 2007 Garden Guide for the Rogue Valley, "The most suitable choices are "plants that need dry, hot summers and moist, but only moderately cold winters."
https://www.grit.com/farm-and-garden/understanding-zones/ for link to zone map
https://www.grit.com/farm-and-garden/understanding-zones/

Characteristics of "Highly Flammable Plants":
-- have low moisture content during fire season.  The moisture content of plants changes by time of day and year, climate and weather, maintenace regime, and more.  Because of a high variability, moisture content alone is not the best indicator of ignition potential. 
-- Plants have waxy or oily leaves or fragrant foliage because of chemical content. 
-- Plant structure is dense or retains dead materials, particularly fine debris.
-- Plants sheds a lot of bark, needles, leaves, or branches that accumlates on, within, or under the plant.
-- Plants have invasive qualities or climb/sprawl creating either horizontally or vertically continuous fuels.
-- Plants are prone to disease causing portions or all of the plant to die.
The area that extends from the home or other structure out 5 ft. No plants or other combustibles should occupy this area. .
The area that extends from 5-10 ft from the home and other flammable structures.  Only fire-resistent plants should be planted in this zone.

The area that extends from 10-30 ft from the home and other flammable structures. Low flammability plants should dominate this zone but some moderately flammable plants may be planted in the area if they are isolated and "properly maintained."
Extends from 30-100 ft from the home. The density and arrangement of zone 2 fire-resistant plants needs to be controlled to break-up horizontal and vertical continuity.
Extends from 50-100ft from the home. Idano Zone 3 rated plants may need to be pruned and thinning regularly to achieve and maintain fire-resistant plant characteristics.
A plant that is able to establish on many sites, grow quickly, and spread to the point of disrupting plant communities or ecosystems.
An invasive species of a plant which is designated as harmful to agricultural crops, ecosystems, or humans or livestock. WSU
An Oregon "native plant" grows in the wild without cultivation in the State of Oregon.  A "naturalized plant" is not considered "native" in this context.

A plant with soft, rather than woody, tissues. Perennials die back to the ground each year but their roots remain alive and send up new top growth each spring. (WSU) A perennial plant or simply perennial is a plant that lives more than two years. The term is often used to differentiate a plant from shorter-lived annuals and biennials. The term is also widely used to distinguish plants with little or no woody growth from trees and shrubs, which are also technically perennials. Wikipedia

In addition to proper siting of plants, it is important to properly maintain landscaping plants by: 
--removing dead vegetation, such as fallen leaves and needles, dead branches, dried grass and dry flowers, prior to and throughout fire season.
--irrigating sufficiently to maintain high plant moisture content during fire season.
--removing low tree branches to a height of at least 10 feet. Also remove small trees and shrubs from under the tree canopy. This will help prevent a surface fire from reaching the tree canopy. Do not remove more than a third of the total tree branches. (UNE)

Trees and shrubs that serve a screening function and are considered fire-resistant  do  not  readily  ignite  from  an  ember  or  other  ignition sources. These plants can be damaged or even killed by fire; however, their foliage and stems  do  not  significantly  contribute  to  the  fuel  load  and  therefore,  the  fire’s  intensity (Simes, 2012).

A woody perennial plant differing from a perennial herb by its persistent and woody stem, and from a tree by its low stature and habit of branching from the base. (WSU) A shrub is a small- to medium-sized perennial woody plant. Unlike herbaceous plants, shrubs have persistent woody stems above the ground. Shrubs can be deciduous or evergreen. They are distinguished from trees by their multiple stems and shorter height, less than 6–10 m tall. Wikipedia

A woody perennial plant, typically having a single stem or trunk growing to a considerable height and bearing lateral branches at some distance from the ground. (WSU)  In botany, a tree is a perennial plant with an elongated stem, or trunk, supporting branches and leaves in most species. In some usages, the definition of a tree may be narrower, including only wood plants with secondary growth, plants that are usable as lumber or plants above a specified height. Wikipedia

A plant having a long, slender stem that trails or creeps on the ground or climbs by winding itself about a support or holding fast with tendrils or claspers. (UW)  A vine is any plant with a growth habit of trailing or scandent stems, lianas or runners. The word vine can also refer to such stems or runners themselves, for instance, when used in wicker work. Wikipedia

Water need may be assumed to mean the relative amount of water a plant needs to survive and reproduce. The amount of water varies seasonally.  In this database, water need is assigned a relative value on a scale from high to very low. Ascription of water need is made by the sources listed.  In general, the sources that identified water need used a graphic such as a bucket with differing water levels or words like high, moderate, moderately-low, low, and very low.
The plant has properties or features that support wildlife generally, or birds, bats, and pollinators specifically as a host* or food source.

0-5 feet if the structure has one-hour flame-resistant siding OR 0-10 feet if the structure has non-flame-resistant siding. In this zone, use plants and materials with very low ignition potential. Plant no trees of shrubs and use only inorganic mulch.
5-30 feet from the house or structure
30 -100 feet from the house or structure

data.title: Introduction
data.description:
WELCOME to Living-with-Fire.org

A portal to information and resource links to help you adapt to life with wildfire

I am a retired federal employee who spent 35 years managing fires and natural and cultural resources in national parks and on public lands. I know that we can't stop the tidal wave of wildfires that are upon us, so I am dedicating my time to trying to help others adapt to life with wildfire.

The subject matter is diverse and there is a wealth of resources available on the Internet which can be time consuming to locate. I created this website to facilitate exploration and learning that ideally leads to collaborative action. I make no claims to completeness, authorship, ownership, or proprietary interest in the materials provided unless specifically indicated. Most of the information is already available through an Internet search.

The original website was created to support a course I instructed in 2020 with Mary Kwart, a retired federal fire management specialist. I want to thank Mary for her contributions to the original site and for allowing me to retain some of her materials.

I want to thank Walter Sydoriak for designing, developing, and maintaining the website. I could not have created this tool without him. I also want to thank Annie Lundgren and the White Rabbit Creator Clubhouse for the monumental work done to create this website and rewrite the Plant List Generator for the web.

Charisse Sydoriak, January 9, 2022, February 12, 2026

Why aren’t existing approaches working? Why are we struggling to engage everyone?     Erica Peng emphasizes that a critical gap in wildfire mitigation isn’t a lack of technical solutions but a human-behavior and psychology barrier: our automatic fight-flight-freeze responses can undermine proactive fire safety actions and collaborative mitigation work. She draws on neuroscience and behavioral psychology to explain why ongoing wildfire threat often triggers stress reactions that make people avoid, deny, or resist preparing for wildfire, even when they know the risks. In this YouTube recording, Peng highlights lessons from organizing fuel reduction and evacuation planning in her own neighborhood, and she points to the importance of understanding and regulating stress reactions to motivate stronger engagement in mitigation and communication among community members and leaders. The study found that strong offshore wind regimes produced fast-moving wildfires characterized by rapid spread, long-distance spotting, and intense firebrand showers, making evacuations difficult and increasing loss of life and property. Although the strongest wind and dry conditions often occurred near Southern California population centers, the most destructive and deadly wildfires in the dataset were in less-populated areas of northern California and western Oregon, where communities were more interspersed with flammable fuels, experienced wind-prone electrical ignitions, and included higher proportions of socially vulnerable residents less able to evade rapidly evolving wildfires."}], "title": "Research Articles on How Fire Spreads in the Built Environment"}, {"links": [{"url": "https://firebrandcollective.org/", "title": "1. Supported by the California Fire Safe Council and working in coordination with CAL FIRE, these councils identify local wildfire hazards and implement practical risk-reduction strategies, but they do not have regulatory authority or enforcement power. Instead, they function as collaborative platforms that mobilize communities to take collective action to improve wildfire preparedness and safety. Fire Safe Council governance, membership, and operations varies by county/city. It is funded by Measure C, a voter-approved parcel tax that provides dedicated revenue for vegetation management, home hardening and defensible space programs, evacuation planning, wildfire detection, and public education. MWPA engages residents through grants, chipper programs, outreach campaigns, and partnerships with nonprofit groups, helping communities take personal mitigation action. While it supports consistent implementation of wildfire safety standards across jurisdictions, enforcement of codes and ordinances remains with the individual member agencies. data.text: Small succulents and low flammability groundcovers less than 1 ft tall may be planted within 1-4 ft of a building, if kept 2 ft apart, watered, maintained free of dead material, and burnable mulches/debris are removed.
data.description: Triggered for plants marked as Zone 0-5 compatible

data.text: Short low flammability plants (<2 ft tall) are a better choice.
data.description: Triggered for plants under 2 ft tall

data.text: Plant this species at least 10 ft away from buildings. Do not grow flammable plants under this plant. On flat ground, space 2 times the mature height of the plant. Increase spacing distance on slopes.
data.description: Triggered for evergreen shrubs

data.text: Remove dead and fine debris often.
data.description: Triggered by Characteristic 5 (debris accumulation) values 1-3

data.text: Do not grow flammable plants under this plant. Prune base branches up 1 to 6 feet depending on plant size. Only grow on burnable mulches that are 10 or more feet from buildings. Remove dead and fine debris often.
data.description: Triggered by Characteristic 7 (base pruning needed) values 1-3

data.text: Papery or loose materials on a plant act as a fuel ladder. Plant at least 10 ft away from buildings.
data.description: Triggered by Characteristic 9 (papery bark) value 1

data.text: Plants with needles or thin, fine flammable leaves should not be used for a hedge. Plant at least 10 ft away from buildings.
data.description: Triggered by Characteristic 11 (thin/needle leaves) values 1-2

data.text: Plant at least 10 ft away from buildings. Do not grow flammable plants under this plant. On flat ground, space 2 times the mature height of the plant. Increase spacing distance on slopes.
data.description: Triggered by Characteristic 13 (dense spacing) values 1-2

data.text: Avoid planting within 5 ft of buildings and take measures not to allow it to spread into the 0-5 ft zone. Only grow on burnable mulches that are 10 or more feet from buildings. Remove dead and fine debris often. Isolate planted areas with pavers, gravels, and similar to limit surface fire spread to buildings and other plants.
data.description: Triggered for groundcovers/annuals/perennials by Characteristics 11A or 11B

data.text: Vines are "ladder" fuels so they should be planted at least 10 ft away from all buildings, trees, and fences. If an evergreen vine, increase distance from buildings. Remove dead and fine debris often.
data.description: Triggered by Characteristic 15 (vine ladder fuel) values 2-4

data.text: Tall, dead, or brown grasses are extremely flammable. Do not plant within 10 ft of a building. Avoid mass plantings within 30 ft of buildings. Mow often to keep 4-inches or less tall during fire season.
data.description: Triggered by Characteristic 16 (grass flammability) values 4-6

data.text: A plant that has waxy leaves, contains volatile chemicals, or retains dead material can burn intensely and explosively. Avoid placing burnable mulch under the plant and remove other plants growing under it. Remove dead and fine debris often. If planted within 30 ft of a building, isolate it or replace with a less flammable plant.
data.description: Triggered by Characteristics 3 (volatile chemicals), 18 (volatile warning), or 20

data.text: There are better plant choices. Consider a plant that is better adapted (often natives), has low flammability, is not highly invasive or messy, uses little water, & does not harm the environment, animals, people or infrastructure.
data.description: Triggered by Characteristics 22A-D (better choices recommendations)

data.text: Mature tree crown is 10 ft away from buildings & 15 ft above the roof. Tree crowns are spaced 10 ft apart. Increase spacing distance on slopes.
data.description: Triggered for trees (deciduous, conifer, or evergreen)
